preFix.title: Waterproofing a shower area with tanking membrane
preFix.subtitle: Apply a waterproof membrane to walls and floor before tiling
preFix.excerpt: How to apply a brushable tanking membrane to a shower enclosure or wetroom area before tiling: preparing the substrate, sealing corners and junctions with tape, building up the membrane coats, and testing for coverage before work continues.
preFix.sourceNotes: Collins Complete DIY Manual. British Standard BS EN ISO 10545-16 (tile adhesive standards). BAL Technical Waterproofing guides (reference, not reproduced).

content.text: A shower enclosure that leaks does not always look wet on the surface. Water migrates slowly behind tiles and through grout joints, reaching the wall
and the floor below. The result is wet joists, failing plasterboard, mould, and structural damage. A
applied before tiling prevents this. Tanking is not optional in a shower enclosure. It is the layer that keeps the structure dry; the tiles and grout alone are not watertight.

A brushable tanking membrane (BAL, Mapei, Wedi, or similar), corner and joint tape (supplied with most membrane systems), a 50 mm brush, a roller for large flat areas, a clean bucket, and a filling knife for pressing tape into corners. Nitrile gloves throughout; the liquid membrane stains skin and clothing.

The substrate must be dry, sound, and free of dust, oil, and loose plaster. On plasterboard walls, repair any damaged areas first. On existing render or plaster, check that it is firmly keyed to the wall and not hollow-sounding. Tap across the surface; a hollow thud indicates loose plaster that must be hacked off and replaced before tanking. Sand any sharp projections flush.

Most tanking systems require a primer coat diluted 1:1 with water, brushed over the entire area and allowed to dry before the membrane is applied. The primer consolidates dusty or powdery substrates and improves adhesion of the membrane. Allow the primer to dry for a minimum of 2 hours in a ventilated room; it should feel tacky but not wet to the touch.
Step 3, tape all corners and junctions

Apply undiluted membrane along all internal corners and at the floor-to-wall junction, using a brush to work it into the angle. Immediately press the joint tape into the wet membrane using the filling knife; the tape should be embedded to its full width with no wrinkles or bubbles. Work one corner at a time and allow the tape section to set for 30 minutes before proceeding. Corners are the most vulnerable point in any waterproofing system.
Step 4, apply the first membrane coat

Once the taped corners are set, apply the first coat of membrane by brush or roller across all walls and the floor in the shower area. Apply a continuous coat without leaving holidays (missed patches). Most systems require a coat thickness of approximately 1 mm; the manufacturer's coverage rate tells you how much product you need for the area. Allow to dry until the surface is a uniform colour (typically pale grey or blue) and feels firm to the touch.

Apply the second coat at 90 degrees to the first (horizontal if the first was vertical) to ensure any missed areas in the first coat are covered. Two full coats are the minimum for a domestic shower. For a wetroom floor with continuous water exposure, three coats are recommended. Allow the final coat to cure for the manufacturer's stated time before tiling; typically 24 hours at 20°C.

attrs.fix: Cut out the failed tape section, reapply primer, allow to tack, press fresh tape into freshly applied membrane, and let set before overcoating.
attrs.cause: Tape was applied to dry membrane rather than wet, or the surface was not primed before the membrane was applied.
attrs.symptom: Membrane peeling away from corner tape

attrs.fix: Apply thinner coats, allow each to dry fully before the next. Two or three thin coats outperform one thick application.
attrs.cause: Coat applied too thick in one pass, causing the surface to skin over while the interior was still wet.
attrs.symptom: Pinhole cracking appearing on membrane surface

attrs.fix: Allow the area to dry completely, cut the membrane back to the corner, reapply tape with fresh membrane, and build up two full overcoats.
attrs.cause: Tape at the floor junction was not fully embedded, leaving a void under the tape.
attrs.symptom: Water seeping at floor-to-wall junction despite membrane

initialFindings.message: PATTERN tutorial has no Method/Steps section and no orderedList — sequential instructions missing
tricolon: tricolon (three parallel items) — two adjectives almost always beats three
unflagged-jargon: "nitrile" — use "protective gloves" unless the specific material is critical to the outcome
paragraph reads at grade 11.6 (qc threshold: 11) — simplify (target grade 6-8)
